text.title: Annual Planning Templates — Guide and Fill Rules
text.summary: Template inventory, fill-order rules, required vs optional sections, and OKR validation criteria.

list.item: annual-plan.md — full year: vision, theme, 3 goals with metrics, strategic priorities, Q1-Q4 breakdown, budget, not-doing list, review schedule
quarterly-review.md — results vs goals, wins, misses with root causes, learnings, adjustments, next-quarter focus
annual-review.md — executive summary, year-by-numbers, accomplishments, challenges, personal reflection
monthly-plan.md — monthly goal, metrics table, week-by-week breakdown, blockers
okr-template.md — objective + why it matters + 3 KRs with baseline and target + initiatives
year-in-review.md — wins with impact, challenges with response, metrics summary, what to do differently

rule.statement: Fill templates in this order: vision (human only) → strategic priorities (human writes, agent critiques) → annual goals (must have numeric baseline and target) → quarterly breakdown → budget table → not-doing list. Never start with the budget or task list.
rule.rationale: Strategy must drive budgets and tasks, not the reverse. Filling budget first anchors the plan to available spend; filling tasks first anchors to current activity. Both undermine strategic clarity.

rule.statement: Every field an agent cannot ground in provided input data must be left as "[NEEDS HUMAN INPUT: reason]". Agents must not invent metrics, goals, or financials when input data is missing.
rule.rationale: Plausible-sounding but invented data in a planning template is more harmful than empty fields. A founder who thinks their CAC is $150 because an LLM generated that number will make wrong budget decisions.

rule.statement: The "Not Doing This Year" section must contain at least 3 concrete items. If a human skips this section, require them to fill it before accepting the plan as complete.
rule.rationale: The stop-doing list is the most value-producing section and the most consistently skipped. Without it, the plan adds new priorities on top of existing commitments, which is how founders burn out.

rule.statement: Reject any Key Result that: (a) uses activity language ("launch", "build", "ship") instead of outcome language, (b) has no numeric target, or (c) lacks a baseline and measurement source. Require a rewrite before accepting.
rule.rationale: Activity KRs measure effort, not impact. A team can ship 5 features and still lose customers. Outcome KRs require the team to think about what "done successfully" looks like from the customer's perspective.

example.note: KR: "Launch a referral program by Q2." — This is an activity, not an outcome. It can be done and fail.
KR: "Increase referral-sourced new customers from 0 to 15/month by end of Q2, measured via Stripe UTM attribution." — Outcome, numeric, baseline, source specified.
